title: Panchatantra Moral Fables Chapter 3 (Expansion Batch 3)
source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925, University of Chicago Press)
revision_note: Panchatantra Expansion Batch 3 (2026-07-23). User asked to cover all remaining Panchatantra reader stories in src/lib/katha-library.ts beyond Batches 1-2. 27 untouched reader ids were reviewed at plot level (not title alone). 12 confident matches were ingested here (items 3.1-3.12), spanning Books 1, 2, 3, and for the first time Book 5. Items 3.1-3.9 (Books 1/3) verbatim text is from theoceanofstories.blogspot.com (Laura Gibbs, Univ. of Oklahoma course site; explicitly attributes Ryder 1925, states public domain), same source used in Batches 1-2. Items 3.10-3.12 (Book 5) verbatim text is from a different source, shreevatsa.net (a Ryder-1925/Hertel-recension HTML export), since theoceanofstories.blogspot.com only ever transcribed Books 1-4 (confirmed by reviewing its full post archive, which contains no Book 5 posts). shreevatsa.net's own copyright page states: Ryder's 1925 translation had its US copyright renewed in 1953, giving a term that (per the page's own 1909/1976/1998-Act analysis, written before 2020) would expire 'at least' by 2020; that date has now passed, and Ryder (d. 1938) has been public domain in life+70 countries including India for decades. Treated as public_domain consistent with the same 1925-publication basis already used for Batches 1-2. 12 stories were explicitly evaluated and SKIPPED, not ingested, because their reader plot did not confidently match the cited Ryder story (do-not-invent-ordering rule): panchatantra-sage-and-mouse (plot is a sage escalating a rescued mouse through cat/dog/tiger forms out of fear/pride; Ryder's only title-adjacent story, 'Mouse-Maid Made Mouse', is a different plot about a mouse-maiden's marriage suitors -- not a match); panchatantra-donkey-and-jackal (cucumber-field/singing-donkey plot does not match Ryder's 'Flop-Ear and Dusty', which is about a jackal luring a donkey to a lion with a false marriage story); panchatantra-greedy-jackal (bowstring-bite plot does not match Ryder's 'Gold's Gloom', which is an unrelated mouse-and-hoarded-treasure story); panchatantra-heron-and-fish and panchatantra-clever-hare-and-elephant (near-duplicate retellings of stories already ingested this batch as crane-and-crab / moon-lake-rabbits -- not re-ingested under a second story_title to avoid double-sourcing the same Ryder text); panchatantra-lion-and-bull (confidently identified as Ryder's Book 1 frametale 'The Loss of Friends', but the frametale is extremely long with dozens of embedded sub-tales and its central discord-sowing/betrayal scene was not fully verified verbatim this session -- deferred, not ingested on a partial/unconfirmed excerpt); panchatantra-lion-mouse-and-cat (no confident Ryder match found; likely a modern composite, not verified as genuine Panchatantra); panchatantra-four-friends (deer/crow/mouse/tortoise frame story; this is the signature Hitopadesha frame tale, not part of Ryder's Panchatantra table of contents -- different source text, not in scope); panchatantra-camel-bell (no confident Ryder match found in the captured table of contents); and the five stories already flagged as skipped in Batch 2's revision_note (panchatantra-dove-king-and-net, panchatantra-fox-and-grapes, panchatantra-king-and-minister, panchatantra-monk-and-jackal, panchatantra-hunter-and-pigeons, panchatantra-raven-and-swan) which remain skipped. panchatantra-two-fish-and-frog was also skipped as a likely variant retelling of 'Forethought, Ready-Wit, and Fatalist' (already ingested in Batch 2 as three-fish).

content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: There was once a heron in a certain place on the edge of a pond. Being old, he sought an easy way of catching fish on which to live. He began by lingering at the edge of his pond, pretending to be quite irresolute, not eating even the fish within his reach. Now among the fish lived a crab. He drew near and said: "Uncle, why do you neglect today your usual meals and amusements?" And the heron replied: "So long as I kept fat and flourishing by eating fish, I spent my time pleasantly... But a great disaster will soon befall you... Today I overheard the talk of a number of fishermen... 'We will try a cast of the net tomorrow or the day after.'" Now when the water-dwellers heard the trickster's report, they all feared for their lives and implored the heron... Then the heron said: "I can transfer you from this pond to another, a bottomless one." By this artful speech they were so led astray... Then the old rascal laughed in his heart... Having thus thought it through, he promised what the thronging fish implored, lifted some in his bill, carried them a certain distance to a slab of stone, and ate them there. Day after day he made the trip... One day the crab, disturbed by the fear of death, importuned him with the words: "Uncle, pray save me, too." And the heron reflected: "I am quite tired of this unvarying fish diet. I should like to taste him." So he picked up the crab and flew through the air. But since he avoided all bodies of water and seemed planning to alight on the sun-scorched rock, the crab asked him: "Uncle, where is that pond without any bottom?" And the heron laughed and said: "Do you see that broad, sun-scorched rock? All the water-dwellers have found repose there. Your turn has now come." Then the crab looked down and saw a great rock of sacrifice, made horrible by heaps of fish-skeletons... "So, before he drops me there, I will catch his neck with all four claws." When he did so, the heron tried to escape, but being a fool, he found no parry to the grip of the crab's nippers, and had his head cut off. Then the crab painfully made his way back to the pond, dragging the heron's neck as if it had been a lotus-stalk. "And that is why I say: A heron ate what fish he could, The bad, indifferent, and good; His greed was never satisfied Till, strangled by a crab, he died."
content.curated_lesson: An old crane could no longer catch fish easily. He stood sadly beside a pond and told the fish, "This pond will dry soon. I can carry you to a larger lake." The frightened fish trusted him. Each day he carried one fish away, but instead of taking it to another lake, he ate it on a rock. At last a crab asked to be carried too. From the crane's neck, the crab saw the bones of the fish below. He tightened his claws around the crane's throat and saved himself. The pond learned that fear makes false helpers powerful.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a certain town lived a merchant named Naduk, who lost his money and determined to travel abroad. In his house was an iron balance-beam inherited from his ancestors, and it weighed a thousand pals. This he put in pawn with Merchant Lakshman before he departed for foreign countries. Now after he had long travelled... he returned to his native city and said to Merchant Lakshman: "Friend Lakshman, return my deposit, the balance-beam." And Lakshman said: "Friend Naduk, your balance beam has been eaten by mice." To this Naduk replied: "Lakshman, you are in no way to blame, if it has been eaten by mice... However, I am going to the river for a bath. Please send your boy Money-God with me." ... So Lakshman's son took the bathing things and delightedly accompanied Naduk to the river. After Naduk had taken his bath, he thrust Lakshman's son Money-God into a mountain cave, blocked the entrance with a great rock, and returned to Lakshman's house. And when Lakshman said: "Friend Naduk, tell me what has become of my son," Naduk answered: "My good Lakshman, a hawk carried him off from the river-bank." "Oh, Naduk!" cried Lakshman. "You liar! How could a hawk possibly carry off a big boy?" "But, Lakshman," retorted Naduk, "the mice could eat a balance-beam made of iron. Give me my balance-beam, if you want your son." Finally, they carried their dispute to the palace gate... the magistrates said to Naduk: "Sir, restore the boy to Lakshman." But Naduk pleaded: "What am I to do? Before my eyes a hawk carried him from the river-bank." "Come, Naduk!" said they, "you are not telling the truth. How can a hawk carry off a fifteen-year-old boy?" Then Naduk laughed outright and said: "Gentlemen, listen to my words. Where mice eat balance-beams of iron A thousand pals in weight, A hawk might steal an elephant; A boy is trifling freight." "How was that?" they asked, and Naduk told them the story of the balance-beam. At this they laughed and caused the restoration of balance-beam and boy to the respective owners.
content.curated_lesson: A merchant stored a heavy iron balance with a friend before leaving town. When he returned and asked for it, the friend lied, "Rats ate your balance." The merchant understood the deceit but remained calm. Later he took the friend's young son for a walk and hid him safely. When asked about the child, he said, "A hawk carried him away." The friend cried, "How can a hawk carry a boy?" The merchant replied, "In a city where rats eat iron, hawks may carry children." The friend returned the balance, and the child was returned safely.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Duel between Elephant and Sparrow
content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a dense bit of jungle lived a sparrow and his wife, who had built their nest on the branch of a tamal tree, and in course of time a family appeared. Now one day a jungle elephant with the spring fever was distressed by the heat, and came beneath that tamal tree in search of shade. Blinded by his fever, he pulled with the tip of his trunk at the branch where the sparrows had their nest, and broke it. In the process the sparrows' eggs were crushed... Then the hen-sparrow lamented, desolate with grief at the death of her chicks. And presently... a woodpecker bird, a great friend of hers, came grieved at her grief... "So if you are my friend, think of some plan to kill this big elephant." ... "Now see what my wit can devise. But you must know that I, too, have a friend, a gnat called Lute-Buzz. I will return with her, so that this villainous beast of an elephant may be killed." So he went with the hen-sparrow, found the gnat... "there is only one possible answer. But I also have a very intimate friend, a frog named Cloud-Messenger. Let us do the right thing by calling him into consultation." So all three went together and told Cloud-Messenger the entire story. And the frog said: "Gnat, you must go and buzz in his fevered ear, so that he may shut his eyes in delight... Then the woodpecker's bill will peck out his eyes. After that I will sit on the edge of a pit and croak. And he, being thirsty, will hear me, and will approach expecting to find a body of water. When he comes to the pit, he will fall in and perish." When they carried out the plan, the fevered elephant shut his eyes in delight at the song of the gnat, was blinded by the woodpecker, wandered thirst-smitten at noonday, followed the croak of a frog, came to a great pit, fell in, and died. "And that is why I say: Woodpecker and sparrow With froggy and gnat, Attacking en masse, laid The elephant flat."
content.curated_lesson: A sparrow had a nest with eggs on a tree branch. An arrogant elephant, drunk with power, shook the tree for sport and broke the eggs. The sparrow wept but did not give up. She asked a woodpecker, a fly, and a frog for help. Each friend had a small skill. The fly distracted the elephant, the woodpecker hurt his eyes, and the frog croaked near a pit so he walked toward the sound and fell. Small allies, united by justice, defeated careless power.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: How the Rabbit Fooled the Elephant
content.book: Book 3, Crows and Owls (inserted tale, within How the Birds Picked a King)
content.text: In a part of a forest lived an elephant-king named Four-Tusk, who had a numerous retinue of elephants... Now once there came a twelve-year drought, so that tanks, ponds, swamps, and lakes went dry... So he sent in eight directions elephants fleet as the wind to search for water. Now those who went east found beside a path near a hermitage a lake named Lake of the Moon... finding a gentle slope all around the lake, the elephants plunged in, thereby crushing the heads, necks, fore-paws and hind-paws of thousands of rabbits who long before had made their home on the banks. ... Then the rabbits who were left alive held an emergency convention... a rabbit named Victory... said compassionately: "Have no fear. They shall not return. I promise it." ... So Victory departed and espied the elephant-king... he climbed upon a tall and jagged rock-pile before saying: "Is it well with you, lord of the two-tusked breed?" ... "I am an envoy," said the rabbit. "In whose service?" asked the elephant... "In the service of the blessèd Moon." ... "Now you have sinfully violated the Lake of the Moon, known afar by my sacred name. And there you have slain rabbits who are under my special protection... Desist from such actions, or great disaster will befall you." ... The rabbit said: "Come, sir, alone. I will point it out." So he went by night to the Lake of the Moon, and showed him the moon reflected in the water... Seeing this, the elephant said: "I purify myself and worship the deity," and he dropped upon the water a trunk... Thereby he disturbed the water, the moon's disk danced to and fro... and he saw a thousand moons. Then Victory started back in great agitation, and said... "You have doubly enraged the Moon... you have touched this water." So the elephant-king, with drooping ears, bowed his head to the very earth in deep obeisance... and he went to his own place. "And that is why I say: The feigning of a great commission Immensely betters your condition: Feigning a message from the moon, The rabbits dwelt in comfort soon."
content.curated_lesson: When elephants damaged the hares' home near a lake, the hares gathered in fear. One small hare volunteered to speak to the elephant king. He climbed a rock and said, "I am a messenger of the Moon, whose lake you have disturbed." He spoke calmly, without insult or panic. The elephant king saw the moon reflected in the water and accepted the warning. The hare saved many lives by using language the powerful would respect.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a certain region was a jackal whose throat was pinched by hunger. While wandering in search of food, he came upon a king's battle ground in the midst of a forest. And as he lingered a moment there, he heard a great sound. This sound troubled his heart exceedingly... "Ah me! Disaster is upon me. I am as good as dead already. Who made that sound?" But on peering about, he spied a war-drum that loomed like a mountain-peak, and he thought: "Was that sound its natural voice, or was it induced from without?" Now when the drum was struck by the tips of grasses swaying in the wind, it made the sound, but was dumb at other times. So he recognized its helplessness, and crept quite near. Indeed, his curiosity led him to strike it himself on both heads, and he became gleeful at the thought: "Aha! After long waiting food comes even to me. For this is sure to be stuffed with meat and fat." Having come to this conclusion, he picked a spot, gnawed a hole, and crept in. And though the leather covering was tough, still he had the luck not to break his teeth. But he was disappointed to find it pure wood and skin... So he backed out, laughing to himself, and said: "I thought at first that it was full Of fat; I crept within And there I did not find a thing Except some wood and skin." And that is why I say that one should not be troubled by a mere sound.
content.curated_lesson: A hungry jackal wandered across an old battlefield. Suddenly he heard a deep booming sound. It echoed through the trees and filled him with fear. At first he wanted to flee, but hunger made him pause. "A sound alone cannot harm me," he thought. He moved carefully toward it. He found a large empty drum. Branches struck it whenever the wind blew. The jackal laughed and learned that many fears are loud because they are hollow.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was once a great tree on a mountain side. On it lived a bird in whose dung gold appeared. One day a hunter came to the spot, and directly in front of him the bird dropped its dung, which at the moment of falling turned to gold. At this the hunter was amazed. "Well, well!" said he. "For eighty years, man and boy, I have had bird-trapping on the brain, and I never once saw gold in a bird's dung." So he set a snare in the tree. And the bird, fool that he was, forgot the danger, and perched on the customary spot. Of course, he was caught immediately. Then the hunter freed him from the snare, put him in a cage and took him home. But he reflected: "What am I to do with this bird of ill omen?... I will take the bird and report to the king myself." And he did so. Now when the king saw the bird, his lotus eyes blossomed and he felt supremely gratified. "Come now, guardsmen," said he. "Look after this bird with anxious care." Then a counsellor said: "He was hatched from an egg. Why keep him? You have no evidence save the mere incredible assurance of a hunter. Is gold ever present in bird-dung? Take this bird from the cage and set him free." So the king, taking the counsellor's advice, freed the bird, who perched on the lofty arch of the doorway long enough to drop dung which was of gold. Then he recited the stanza: I played the fool at first; then he Who had me on his tether; And then the king and counsellor - We all were fools together. After which he took his carefree flight through the atmosphere.
content.curated_lesson: A hunter caught a strange bird whose droppings turned into gold. Amazed, he feared keeping it secretly and presented it to the king. The minister laughed, "No bird can produce gold. This is a trick." Without testing properly, the king released the bird. The bird flew to a branch, dropped gold once more, and said, "First the hunter was foolish, then the minister, then the king." Fortune leaves when judgment is absent.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Weaver Who Loved a Princess
content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In the Molasses Belt is a city called Sugarcane City. In it lived two friends, a weaver and a carpenter... One day there was a great festival... they caught a glimpse of a princess seated at the window of a stucco palace... And the weaver, ravished by lavish loveliness... tottered home... At last the carpenter said: "Rise, bathe, eat. Say farewell to despondency. I will invent something such that you will enjoy with her the delights of love without loss of time." ... the carpenter came bringing a brand-new mechanical bird, like Garuda, the bird of Vishnu... "disguise yourself as Vishnu... mount this Garuda bird, alight on the maidens' balcony of the palace." ... All at once she spied the weaver, disguised as Vishnu and mounted on his heavenly bird. At sight of him she started from her bed, adored his feet... "Nay, you have been my bride, now fallen to earth by reason of a curse... I will now wed you by the ceremony used in heaven." ... One day the guards at the women's quarters observed indications that the princess was meeting a man... the princess, with shamefaced, drooping glances, recounted the whole story of the weaver disguised as Vishnu. ... At this juncture envoys arrived to collect the yearly tribute for King Valour... the king showed them his bare bottom. And they returned to their own country... Then the southern monarch... marched against the king... Meanwhile the weaver... took counsel with his brooding mind: "What am I to do now?... It is better, then, to die game." ... Hereupon the weaver, inspired by Vishnu... the weaver hurled his discus at Valour. This discus, after cutting the king in twain, returned to his hand. At the sight, all the kings... implored him who bore the form of Vishnu... "And that is why I say: The gods befriend a man who climbs Determination's height: So Vishnu, discus, bird sustained The weaver in the fight."
content.curated_lesson: A poor weaver loved a princess and knew he could never meet her honestly. With a friend's help, he made a wooden Garuda and dressed as Vishnu to visit her at night. The princess believed him divine. But when the kingdom faced war, the king begged the "Lord" to protect them. The weaver trembled because his disguise had become a public burden. Ashamed yet trapped, he went to battle and fought with unexpected courage. The story warns that deception may bring a role one is not ready to carry.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was once an elderly black snake in a certain spot, and his name was Slow-Poison... he went to a pond containing many frogs, and behaved as if very dejected. As he waited thus, a frog came to the edge of the water and asked: "Uncle, why don't you bustle about today for food as usual?" "My dear friend," said Slow-Poison, "I am afflicted... I bit [a Brahman boy's toe by mistake], and the boy died immediately. Then the sorrowing father cursed me... 'You shall for this sin become a vehicle for frogs, and shall subsist on whatever they choose to allow you.' Consequently, I have come here to serve as your vehicle." Now the frog reported this to all the others... the frog-king, whose name was Water-Foot... climbed upon Slow-Poison's hood. The others also... climbed on his back... The next day, Slow-Poison was wily enough to move very slowly. So Water-Foot said: "My dear Slow-Poison, why don't you carry us nicely, as you did before?" And Slow-Poison said: "O King, I have no carrying power today because of lack of food." "My dear fellow," said the king, "eat the plebeian frogs." When Slow-Poison heard this, he quivered with joy... So he ate frogs uninterruptedly, and in a very few days he grew strong... Why spin it out? He ate them all so completely that not even frog-seed was left. "And that is why I say: Bear even foes upon your back; When fortune clogs Your path, endure. The great black snake Slew many frogs."
content.curated_lesson: An old snake could not hunt well. He told the frogs, "I am cursed to serve frogs as a carrier." The frog king was delighted and rode on his back. The snake moved slowly and gained trust. Then he said, "I am weak from hunger. May I eat one frog a day?" The proud king agreed carelessly. Soon the pond grew empty. The king realised too late that comfort bought with another's suffering eventually reaches one's own door.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a certain city lived two friends, sons of merchants, and their names were Right-Mind and Wrong-Mind. These two travelled to another country far away in order to earn money. There the one named Right-Mind... found a pot containing a thousand dinars... When they drew near their native city, Right-Mind said: "My good friend, a half of this falls to your share." But Wrong-Mind... said: "...Let us each take a hundred dinars, and go to our homes after burying the remainder." ... Within a year this, too, had slipped... he went alone, removed the treasure, and levelled the ground. A mere month later, he took the initiative, going to Right-Mind and saying: "My good friend, let us divide the rest of the money equally." So he and Right-Mind visited the spot and began to dig. When the excavation failed to reveal any treasure, that impudent Wrong-Mind first of all smote his own head with the empty pot, then shouted: "What became of that good lucre? Surely, Right-Mind, you must have stolen it." ... together they carried their dispute to court... "I have a witness, the goddess of the wood." ... Then Wrong-Mind went home and asked his father's help... "This very night I am going to hide you out of sight in a hole in the mimosa tree... In the morning you must be my witness." ... Wrong-Mind... hid his father out of sight in the hole in the tree. When morning came... he cried in piercing tones: "...O blessèd goddess of the wood, which of us two is the thief? Speak." Then Wrong-Mind's father spoke from his hole in the mimosa: "Gentlemen, Right-Mind took that money." ... Meanwhile Right-Mind heaped inflammable matter about the hole in the mimosa and set fire to it. As the mimosa burned, Wrong-Mind's father issued from the hole with a pitiful wail, his body scorched... And they all asked: "Why, sir! What does this mean?" "It is all Wrong-Mind's doing," he replied. Whereupon the king's men hanged Wrong-Mind to a branch of the mimosa, while they commended Right-Mind... "And that is why I say: Right-Mind was one, and Wrong-Mind two; I know the tale by heart: The son in smoke made father choke By being super-smart."
content.curated_lesson: Two friends, Dharmabuddhi and Papabuddhi, earned money together and hid it under a tree. Later Papabuddhi secretly stole the money. When they returned, he accused Dharmabuddhi. He even hid his father inside the hollow tree to speak as a false witness. The judge ordered smoke into the hollow, and the hidden father came out coughing. The lie was exposed. A false witness traps the one who arranges it.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was once a Brahman named Godly in a certain town. His wife mothered a single son and a mungoose. And as she loved little ones, she cared for the mungoose also like a son, giving him milk from her breast, and salves, and baths, and so on. But she did not trust him, for she thought: "A mungoose is a nasty kind of creature. He might hurt my boy." One day she tucked her son in bed, took a waterjar, and said to her husband: "Now, Professor, I am going for water. You must protect the boy from the mungoose." But when she was gone, the Brahman went off somewhere himself to beg food, leaving the house empty. While he was gone, a black snake issued from his hole and, as fate would have it, crawled toward the baby's cradle. But the mungoose, feeling him to be a natural enemy, and fearing for the life of his baby brother, fell upon the vicious serpent halfway, joined battle with him, tore him to bits, and tossed the pieces far and wide. Then, delighted with his own heroism, he ran, blood trickling from his mouth, to meet the mother; for he wished to show what he had done. But when the mother saw him coming, saw his bloody mouth and his excitement, she feared that the villain must have eaten her baby boy, and without thinking twice, she angrily dropped the water-jar upon him, which killed him the moment that it struck. There she left him without a second thought, and hurried home, where she found the baby safe and sound, and near the cradle a great black snake, torn to bits. Then, overwhelmed with sorrow because she had thoughtlessly killed her benefactor, her son, she beat her head and breast. At this moment the Brahman came home with a dish of rice gruel... and saw his wife bitterly lamenting her son, the mungoose. "Greedy! Greedy!" she cried. "Because you did not do as I told you, you must now taste the bitterness of a son's death, the fruit of the tree of your own wickedness."
content.curated_lesson: A Brahmin family kept a young mongoose in their home. The mongoose grew up with their baby and loved the child deeply. The mother trusted him, but a small doubt remained in her heart because he was still a wild creature. One day she left the baby sleeping and went to fetch water. While she was gone, a snake entered the house and moved toward the cradle. The mongoose attacked the snake and killed it after a fierce struggle. When the mother returned, the mongoose ran to greet her with blood on his mouth. Without entering the room or checking the child, she cried, "You have killed my baby!" In blind fear, she struck him down. Then she rushed inside and found her child safe, sleeping peacefully. Beside the cradle lay the dead snake. The mongoose had protected the baby with his life. The mother wept bitterly, but regret could not undo the act done in haste.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
content.revision_note: Book 5 text sourced from shreevatsa.net (Ryder-1925/Hertel-recension HTML export), not theoceanofstories.blogspot.com, because that blog never transcribed Book 5. See manifest-level revision_note for the rights-status basis (1925 publication, renewed-copyright term expired 'at least' by 2020 per the source page's own analysis, plus author d. 1938 life+70 public domain in most other countries).

content.text: In a certain town were four Brahmans who lived in friendship. Three of them had reached the far shore of all scholarship, but lacked sense. The other found scholarship distasteful; he had nothing but sense. One day they met for consultation. "What is the use of attainments," said they, "if one does not travel, win the favor of kings, and acquire money? Whatever we do, let us all travel." But when they had gone a little way, the eldest of them said: "One of us, the fourth, is a dullard, having nothing but sense. Now nobody gains the favorable attention of kings by simple sense without scholarship. Therefore we will not share our earnings with him. Let him turn back and go home." ... With this agreement they continued their journey, and in a forest they found the bones of a dead lion. Thereupon one of them said: "A good opportunity to test the ripeness of our scholarship. Here lies some kind of creature, dead. Let us bring it to life by means of the scholarship we have honestly won." Then the first said: "I know how to assemble the skeleton." The second said: "I can supply skin, flesh, and blood." The third said: "I can give it life." So the first assembled the skeleton, the second provided skin, flesh, and blood. But while the third was intent on giving the breath of life, the man of sense advised against it, remarking: "This is a lion. If you bring him to life, he will kill every one of us." "You simpleton!" said the other, "it is not I who will reduce scholarship to a nullity." "In that case," came the reply, "wait a moment, while I climb this convenient tree." When this had been done, the lion was brought to life, rose up, and killed all three. But the man of sense, after the lion had gone elsewhere, climbed down and went home. "And that is why I say: Scholarship is less than sense..."
content.curated_lesson: Four friends travelled together. Three were learned scholars with great technical knowledge. The fourth had little book learning but strong common sense. They found bones in the forest. The scholars assembled them into a lion, gave it flesh, and prepared to give it life. The practical friend warned them, but they laughed. He climbed a tree. The lion came alive and attacked the scholars. Knowledge without wisdom had created its own destroyer.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
content.revision_note: Book 5 text sourced from shreevatsa.net; see manifest-level revision_note for source/rights basis.

content.text: In a certain town lived a Brahman named Seedy, who got some barley-meal by begging, ate a portion, and filled a jar with the remainder. This jar he hung on a peg one night, placed his cot beneath it, and fixing his gaze on the jar, fell into a hypnotic reverie. "Well, here is a jar full of barley-meal," he thought. "Now if famine comes, a hundred rupees will come out of it. With that sum I will get two she-goats. Every six months they will bear two more she-goats. After goats, cows. When the cows calve, I will sell the calves. After cows, buffaloes; after buffaloes, mares. From the mares I shall get plenty of horses. The sale of these will mean plenty of gold. The gold will buy a great house with an inner court. Then someone will come to my house and offer his lovely daughter with a dowry. She will bear a son, whom I shall name Moon-Lord. When he is old enough to ride on my knee, I will take a book, sit on the stable roof, and think. Just then Moon-Lord will see me, will jump from his mother's lap in his eagerness to ride on my knee, and will go too near the horses. Then I shall get angry and tell my wife to take the boy. But she will be busy with her chores and will not pay attention to what I say. Then I will get up and kick her." Being sunk in his hypnotic dream, he let fly such a kick that he smashed the jar. And the barley-meal which it contained turned him white all over. "And that is why I say: Do not indulge in hopes..."
content.curated_lesson: A poor Brahmin received a pot of flour and hung it above his bed. Looking at it, he imagined selling it, buying goats, becoming rich, marrying, and having children. In the dream, he imagined scolding a disobedient child. He kicked his foot to show authority. His foot struck the real pot. It shattered, and the flour spilled everywhere. His future palace vanished because he neglected the present vessel.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
content.revision_note: Book 5 text sourced from shreevatsa.net; see manifest-level revision_note for source/rights basis. Note: Ryder's original has the dreamer imagining kicking his wife (not a 'disobedient child' as in the Shoonaya curated_lesson paraphrase); the verbatim translation text above preserves Ryder's actual wording rather than the softened paraphrase.
